bare: - Palmer, C. J., Lawson, R. P., & Hohwy, J. (2017). Bayesian approaches to autism. *Psychological Bulletin*, 143(5), 521-542. [APA (paywalled)](https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000097)

claim_context: Treated in the hub as 'THE key study on prosodic information-structure marking in autism' with two subgroups showing opposite patterns.
source_says: PMC3312270 actually resolves to DePape, Chen, Hall, & Trainor (2012), 'Use of Prosody and Information Structure in High Functioning Adults with Autism in Relation to Language Ability,' Frontiers in Psychology — NOT Paul, Augustyn, Klin & Volkmar (2005) JADD.
claim_support: MISATTRIBUTION. The title and subgroup findings the hub describes belong to DePape et al. 2012, not Paul et al. 2005. The hub's interpretation of the study is consistent with the DePape paper, so the CLAIM is supported but the citation metadata (author, year, journal) is wrong.
notes: AUTHOR/YEAR MISATTRIBUTION. Fix: re-cite as DePape, Chen, Hall, & Trainor (2012), Frontiers in Psychology 3:72, PMC3312270. This is the dominant slice-9 error pattern.

bare: - **Pearson et al.'s (2023) internalization findings** (*"I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic"*) document that autistic adults who have been harmed by people they initially trusted frequently arrive at the conviction that they are misreading the harm, and one of the factors that makes the misreading stick is the presence of allyship-adjacent features in the harmer's self-presentation.
claim_context: Uses Pearson et al. 2023 internalization findings to support the claim that allyship-adjacent features help internalization stick.
source_says: Pearson/Rose/Rees 2023 reports internalization of harm by autistic survivors, and discusses that harmers were often people the survivor trusted.
claim_support: Broad framing is supported. The specific 'allyship-adjacent features' framing is an interpretive extension by the hub author of the qualitative data.
notes: The Pearson paper does not explicitly theorize 'allyship-adjacent features' — this is the hub's framing layered on top of the qualitative findings.

bare: Pearson, Rose & Rees (2023) titled their qualitative study "'I felt like I deserved it because I was autistic'" because that phrase, or close variants, recurred across their interview participants. The phrase is the endpoint of a successful internalization: the autistic person no longer needs the abuser to discount them, because they are doing it themselves. The diagnostic frame has become the operating premise.
claim_context: Used as narrative attribution for the title/phrase of the Pearson 2023 paper.
source_says: The paper's title does come from recurring participant phrasing — this is accurate.
claim_support: Directly supports the hub's interpretive framing.

bare: - **Pijnacker et al. (2009)** tested 11 adults with autistic disorder, 17 with Asperger syndrome, and 28 controls. The combined ASD group was **just as likely as controls to derive scalar implicatures**. However, there was a difference between the autistic disorder and Asperger syndrome subgroups, suggesting heterogeneity within the spectrum. Published in *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 39(4), 607-618. PubMed ID: 19052858
claim_context: Narrative use of Pijnacker 2009 with specific N and findings.
source_says: All details (N, subgroup heterogeneity, issue info, PubMed ID) are correct.

bare: - Raymaker et al. (2020). Autistic Burnout. — [PMC7313636](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7313636/)

bare: Raymaker et al. (2020) defined autistic burnout as a distinct construct precisely because it does not map onto the existing categories of laziness, depression, or weakness. The chronic exhaustion is qualitatively different from depression. The skill loss is qualitatively different from forgetting or carelessness. The reduced sensory tolerance is not psychosomatic.
claim_context: Narrative use of Raymaker 2020 to argue that burnout is a distinct construct.
source_says: Raymaker et al. 2020 explicitly positions autistic burnout as distinct from depression and similar constructs.

bare: Raymaker et al. (2020) defined autistic burnout as characterized by chronic exhaustion, loss of skills, and reduced sensory tolerance, attributed to chronic life stress and capacity mismatch. The overlap between autistic burnout and complex trauma is significant:
claim_context: Characterization of the Raymaker 2020 definition.
source_says: Matches the paper's definition.

bare: Raymaker et al. (2020) defined autistic burnout from community-based participatory research with 19 interviews and 19 public online sources. The primary characteristics they identified:
claim_context: Methodological attribution: 19 interviews + 19 online sources.

key: raymaker|2020|having all of your internal re
bare: - Raymaker, D. M., et al. (2020). "'Having All of Your Internal Resources Exhausted Beyond Measure and Being Left with No Clean-Up Crew': Defining Autistic Burnout." *Autism in Adulthood* 2(2): 132–143. PMC7313636. (Reduced sensory tolerance and skill loss are primary characteristics; both are conditions that make meltdowns more frequent.)
claim_context: Full form with the paper's actual full title.
source_says: Verified.

bare: **Raymaker et al. (2020)** on autistic burnout provides the cost substrate: sustained masking is one of the primary precipitants of autistic burnout, a condition characterized by loss of skills, loss of executive function, and loss of the capacity for the masking itself. The burnout profile is the structural consequence of the third leg of the double bind operating across years. [VALIDATED]
claim_context: Uses Raymaker 2020 to argue that sustained masking precipitates burnout.
source_says: Raymaker 2020 identifies masking as a major precipitant of burnout.
